---
title: `[___]`
author: `[___]`
date: today
format: pdf
jupyter: python3
bibiliography: `[___]`
---

# Introduction
This document is a first introduction for learning the basics of Quarto. Your task is to fill out the `[___]` spots with the correct values.

# Plot
Fill in the gaps from the plot.

```{python}
#| label: `[___]`
#| fig-cap: `[___]`
#| echo: `[___]`

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

weeks = np.arange(1, 11)
jupyter = np.array([2, 3, 5, 6, 8, 7, 9, 8, 9, 10])
quarto  = np.array([6, 5, 4, 3, 3, 2, 2, 1, 1, 1])

plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4))
plt.plot(weeks, jupyter, marker="o", color="tomato", linewidth=2, label="Jupyter")
plt.plot(weeks, quarto, marker="o", color="steelblue", linewidth=2, label="Quarto")
plt.xlabel("Weeks")
plt.ylabel("Frustration (1-10)")
plt.title("Frustration over time")
plt.legend()
plt.ylim(0, 11)
plt.xticks(weeks)
plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

A plot can be referenced in text. Use this feature to reference the plot from above using the label `[___]`.

# Citation
You can cite references from your references.bib file.

The study compared the jump height between dog and cat fleas `[___]`.

# References
